food_subgroup: Deep-fried banana fritter in rice flour batter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for goreng pisang as a prepared hawker snack. Closest parent would be a deep-fried fruit fritter concept; not yet represented in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KCA is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a deep-fried fruit fritter. No exact code exists for goreng pisang specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for goreng pisang as a prepared food item.

main_ingredients: palm olein or canola frying oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving of goreng pisang is 2 pieces, each being one halved pisang raja banana coated in rice flour batter and deep-fried, totalling approximately 120g.

nutrient_source.title: Goreng Pisang — HPB Singapore Foodo + USDA FoodData Central Component Calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for goreng pisang, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for raw pisang raja banana and rice flour batter components. Values represent the cooked composite per 100g as sold at Singapore hawker stalls.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Banana and Deep-Fried Battered Fruit — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pisang raja banana GI is approximately 48–52 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for ripe banana varieties. The rice flour and wheat flour batter adds refined starch, raising the composite GI to approximately 55. Deep-frying fat moderates absorption rate. GL per 120g serving calculated as: (55 × 40g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.0. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for goreng pisang as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~80mg per 120g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Goreng pisang is among the lowest-sodium Singapore hawker snacks at approximately 80mg sodium per 2-piece serving; potassium (~336mg per 120g) from the banana further supports blood pressure management via K:Na antagonism, making it suitable for hypertensive individuals in moderate portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~22 per 2-piece serving) from combined banana sugar and batter starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 (low) and a GL of ~22 per 2-piece serving, goreng pisang delivers a moderate carbohydrate load (~40g per 120g) from banana natural sugars and refined flour batter; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece and pair with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.8g per 120g serving) and zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Goreng pisang contains approximately 1.8g saturated fat and 0g trans fat per 2-piece serving when fried at correct temperature (175–185°C) with palm olein or canola oil; the low saturated fat content and absence of trans fat make it a relatively heart-friendly hawker snack when consumed occasionally and in standard 2-piece portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goreng Pisang (Banana Fritter) is a quintessential Singapore and Malaysian hawker street snack consisting of pisang raja banana halves dipped in a light batter of rice flour and wheat flour, then deep-fried in palm olein or canola oil until golden and crispy. The result is a contrast between a crunchy, lightly battered exterior and a warm, soft, naturally sweet banana interior. Pisang raja is preferred over Cavendish banana for its starchier texture and slightly lower glycemic index. A standard hawker serving is 2 pieces (~120g), providing approximately 250 kcal, 1.7g protein per 100g, 8.3g fat per 100g, and 33.3g carbohydrate per 100g. Sodium is very low at ~67mg per 100g (~80mg per 2-piece serving), making goreng pisang one of the lowest-sodium Singapore hawker snacks. The GI is estimated at ~55 (low per University of Sydney GI classification: low ≤55) with a glycemic load of ~22 per serving. Potassium from the banana (~280mg per 100g) supports blood pressure management. The snack is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It is sold at dedicated goreng pisang stalls at wet markets, bus interchanges, and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, typically as a breakfast or afternoon tea-time snack.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. The food is a well-documented Singapore hawker staple with consistent preparation methods. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database banana entries, clearly documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for goreng pisang as a composite cooked dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level fatty acid data for palm olein frying oil absorption in this specific preparation is not reported. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the cooked composite dish is not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB Singapore source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegan snack with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 as this is a vegan food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB Singapore source; banana and rice flour batter contain negligible vitamin D. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for ripe banana (GI ~48–52) adjusted upward for refined flour batter contribution; no direct GI measurement for goreng pisang as a composite dish exists. gi_category is 'low': per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 = low, GI 56–69 = medium, GI ≥70 = high; a GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. Tamil local name (வாழைப்பழ பஜ்ஜி) and Hindi local name (केले का पकोड़ा) are functional descriptive equivalents used in Singapore Tamil and Hindi communities; no single canonical term exists.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'low' to 'medium' based on assumption that GI 55 = medium.

Iteration 3: Reverted gi_category back to 'low' with explicit documentation. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification standard, GI ≤55 is classified as LOW. A GI of exactly 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. The validator feedback from iteration 2 was based on an incorrect classification assumption. The authoritative University of Sydney scale defines low as ≤55, medium as 56–69, high as ≥70. gi_source notes updated to explicitly state this classification basis. This resolves the Q4 discrepancy definitively.
